steps.title: How does a shop know 47 people came in today?

narration.default: Walk into any big shop and you'll see a tiny sensor pointing across the doorway. By the end of the day, the manager knows exactly how many people came in. Forty-seven. One thousand and twelve. Whatever the number is. Today you'll build that exact thing — a sensor that watches a doorway, a display that shows the count. And along the way you'll meet edge detection, which is the single most important pattern in all of embedded programming. Make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode, and the TM1637 7-Segment extension is added in Sensor Manager. If adding extensions is new to you, the Move a Servo tutorial walks you through Sensor Manager.

You know the drill by now. Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. Setup runs once when the program starts. Loop runs forever, over and over, while the Arduino has power.

steps.title: Listen for the sensor on pin 2

narration.default: Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 2 and mode to input pullup. Pin 2 will listen for the doorway sensor. The sensor we're using is an IR break-beam — it shines invisible infrared light across the doorway. When the doorway is empty, the beam is clear and pin 2 reads high. When someone walks through, they break the beam and pin 2 drops to low.

We need somewhere to show the count. A 7-segment display is one of those red-glowing digit panels you've seen on microwaves and digital clocks — each digit is built from seven little bars that light up in different combinations. Drag the setup 7-segment block into setup. Set the clock pin to 3 and the data pin to 4. Those two pins are how the Arduino tells the display which digits to show.

steps.title: The naive way — count every loop

narration.default: Here's the obvious way to write a counter. Every time loop runs, check the sensor. If it's low — beam broken — add one to the count. Done, right? Wrong. Loop runs thousands of times per second. If someone stands in the doorway for two seconds, the beam stays broken the whole time — and your count goes up by hundreds or thousands. One person walking past would register as a stampede. So the obvious way is broken. We need a smarter idea.

Here's the smart idea. Don't count when the beam is broken. Count only when the beam JUST got broken — when the sensor transitioned from high to low. That single transition is one person stepping into the doorway. After that, even if they stand there forever, the sensor stays low but it isn't transitioning anymore, so we don't count again. This trick has a name: edge detection. The moment of change is called the edge. Once you've learned this pattern, you'll see it everywhere — every button click, every doorbell, every keypress, every step counter on a phone — they all use edge detection underneath.

To detect a transition, we need to compare two things: what the sensor is reading right now, and what it was reading the last time loop ran. If now is low and before was high — that's the edge. That's the moment a person crossed the beam. So we need two memory boxes. One called currentState for what the sensor says now. One called lastState for what it said last loop. We'll also need a third box to hold the actual count. Three boxes in total.

Open the Variables category and click Make a Variable. Name it count. Then drag a set count to block into setup, and leave the value as 0. The shop opens for the day with zero people counted — every counter starts at zero.

Click Make a Variable again. Name this one lastState. Drag a set lastState to block into setup, and drop a high block from the Arduino category as its value. We start by assuming the doorway is empty — beam clear, sensor high — so lastState begins as high.

One more. Click Make a Variable, name it currentState. We don't need to set it in setup — it'll be filled in every loop with the freshest sensor reading. Three boxes done: count, lastState, currentState.

Drag a set currentState to block into the loop section. Drop a digitalRead block from the Arduino category as its value, with pin set to 2. Now every time loop runs, currentState gets the freshest reading from the doorway sensor — low if someone is breaking the beam, high if the doorway is clear.

Now the heart of edge detection. Drag an if block into the loop, below the sensor read. Drop an and block from the Operators category into its diamond slot. The and block needs both halves to be true before the if runs. Build the left half: currentState equals low. Build the right half: lastState equals high. In plain words: if the beam is broken right now and it was clear last time — somebody just walked through.

Inside the if branch, drag a set count to block. Drop an addition block from the Operators category as its value. Put the count variable on the left side of the plus, and the number 1 on the right. So: set count to count plus 1. Whatever count was, add one to it. This line only runs when the edge condition is true — exactly once per person walking through.

Still inside the if branch, drag a display 7-segment show block. Drop the count variable into its number slot. Every time the count goes up by one, this line pushes the new total out to the display. The little red digits update — 1, 2, 3, 4 — as people walk past.

steps.title: Remember this reading for next loop

narration.default: This step is the most important one in the whole tutorial — miss it and the counter breaks. Drag a set lastState to block into the loop, after the if block, not inside it. Drop the currentState variable as its value. So at the end of every loop, whatever the sensor was reading right now becomes the new lastState for next time. Without this line, lastState stays low forever, and the counter would never stop ticking up. With it, lastState tracks currentState exactly one step behind — which is what makes edge detection work.

At the very end of the loop, drag a delay block. Set it to 50 milliseconds. This gives the loop a steady rhythm — twenty checks per second, plenty fast to catch someone walking past, but slow enough that the Arduino isn't pegging itself for no reason. Every loop should have a small pause at the end. The blocks are done — let's wire up the simulator.

Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose Push Button. The simulator doesn't yet have a real IR break-beam sensor component, so we'll use a push button as a stand-in. The behaviour is identical to a real beam: pin 2 reads high when nothing is pressing the button — that's the beam clear, doorway empty. Pin 2 drops to low when the button is pressed — beam broken, person walking through. In the pin dialog, set pin to 2 and click Save.

Click the plus button again and choose 7-Segment. In the pin dialog, set the clock pin to 3 and the data pin to 4 — matching what your setup block declared. Click Save. The virtual display appears next to the virtual Arduino, ready to show numbers.

Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The display lights up showing zero. The sensor sits quietly, waiting for someone to walk past.

Press the push button in the simulator and release — that's one person walking through the beam. The display ticks up to one. Press and release again — second person. The display says two. Each press adds exactly one to the count, no matter how long you hold the button. Try holding the button for ten seconds — the count stays put, because there's no new edge while the button is held. The next press fires another edge. That's the magic of edge detection working.

Look at what you just learned. Edge detection — the pattern that handles every did-this-just-change problem in embedded code. Multiple variables working together — count holding the total, currentState holding now, lastState holding before. State — the idea that a program can remember what the world looked like a moment ago and compare it to the world now. The 7-segment display — how programs put numbers in front of human eyes. The shape of this tutorial — sensor, edge, count, display — runs every footfall counter in every shop, every turnstile in every metro station, every tap counter on every kitchen sink, every step counter in every fitness band. You can write them now.
